title: BERT
definition: A transformer-based language model by Google that pre-trains deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context in all layers.
why_used: BERT revolutionized NLP by enabling transfer learning — one pre-trained model can be fine-tuned for dozens of downstream tasks with minimal task-specific architecture changes.
interview_point: BERT is encoder-only (bidirectional context). It uses two pre-training objectives: Masked Language Modeling (MLM — predict masked tokens) and Next Sentence Prediction (NSP — predict if two sentences follow each other). [CLS] token is used for classification tasks.
advantages: Fine-tuned in minutes on downstream tasks
real_world_example:
```python
from transformers import BertTokenizer, BertForSequenceClassification
import torch

# Load pretrained BERT
tokenizer = BertTokenizer.from_pretrained('bert-base-uncased')
model = BertForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained(
    'bert-base-uncased',
    num_labels=2  # Binary classification
)

# Tokenize input
inputs = tokenizer(
    'The movie was fantastic!',
    return_tensors='pt',
    max_length=128,
    truncation=True,
    padding='max_length'
)

# Fine-tune or inference
outputs = model(**inputs)
logits = outputs.logits
predicted_class = torch.argmax(logits, dim=1)
```

title: RoBERTa
definition: A Facebook AI Research model that improves BERT's pretraining by using more data, larger batches, longer training, removing NSP objective, and using dynamic masking (mask changes each epoch).
why_used: Outperforms BERT on most benchmarks with the same architecture but better training. The go-to encoder model for classification and NER tasks.
interview_point: Key differences from BERT: 1) Removes NSP (Next Sentence Prediction) — researchers found it didn't help. 2) Uses dynamic masking (new mask each epoch vs BERT's static masking). 3) Trained on 10x more data. 4) Uses byte-pair encoding (BPE) not WordPiece.
advantages: Outperforms BERT on most NLU benchmarks
limitations: Same limitations as BERT (encoder-only, 512 token limit)
real_world_example:
```python
from transformers import RobertaTokenizer, RobertaForSequenceClassification

tokenizer = RobertaTokenizer.from_pretrained('roberta-base')
model = RobertaForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained('roberta-base', num_labels=3)

inputs = tokenizer('I love this product!', return_tensors='pt',
                   truncation=True, max_length=512, padding='max_length')
outputs = model(**inputs)
```

title: DistilBERT
definition: A smaller, faster, cheaper version of BERT trained via knowledge distillation. Retains 97% of BERT's language understanding while being 40% smaller and 60% faster.
why_used: When you need BERT-level performance but with latency and memory constraints — ideal for production deployment and mobile applications.
interview_point: Knowledge Distillation: a smaller 'student' model is trained to mimic the outputs (soft probabilities) of a larger 'teacher' model (BERT). The student learns from both the hard labels AND the teacher's soft probability distribution, which contains more information than one-hot labels.
real_world_example:
```python
from transformers import pipeline

# Use DistilBERT for fast inference
classifier = pipeline(
    'sentiment-analysis',
    model='distilbert-base-uncased-finetuned-sst-2-english'
)
result = classifier('This product is amazing!')
print(result)  # [{'label': 'POSITIVE', 'score': 0.999}]
```

title: Word2Vec
definition: A word embedding model that learns dense vector representations of words where semantically similar words are close in vector space. Uses two architectures: CBOW (predicts center word from context) and Skip-gram (predicts context from center word).
why_used: Converts words to numeric vectors capturing semantic relationships. The famous example: vector(King) - vector(Man) + vector(Woman) ≈ vector(Queen) demonstrates semantic arithmetic.
interview_point: Skip-gram: better for rare words (treats each (center, context) pair separately). CBOW: faster training (averages context to predict center). Word2Vec produces STATIC embeddings — same word always gets same vector regardless of context (unlike BERT's contextual embeddings).
advantages: Works well as feature input for downstream models
limitations: Static embeddings (same vector regardless of context)
Cannot handle polysemy (same word, different meanings)
real_world_example:
```python
from gensim.models import Word2Vec

# Tokenized sentences
sentences = [['machine', 'learning', 'is', 'great'],
             ['deep', 'learning', 'is', 'part', 'of', 'machine', 'learning']]

model = Word2Vec(
    sentences,
    vector_size=100,   # Embedding dimensions
    window=5,          # Context window size
    min_count=1,       # Minimum word frequency
    sg=1,              # 1=Skip-gram, 0=CBOW
    epochs=10,
    workers=4
)

# Semantic similarity
similarity = model.wv.similarity('machine', 'learning')
print(f'Similarity: {similarity:.4f}')

# Most similar words
model.wv.most_similar('machine', topn=5)
```

title: TF-IDF
definition: A numerical statistic reflecting how important a word is to a document in a corpus. TF (Term Frequency) measures how often a word appears in a document; IDF (Inverse Document Frequency) penalizes common words that appear in many documents.
why_used: Creates meaningful sparse vector representations of text without neural networks. Excellent baseline for document classification and information retrieval.
interview_point: IDF = log(N / df(t)) where N = total documents, df(t) = documents containing term t. Common words like 'the' have low IDF (appear in all docs). Rare but informative words have high IDF. TF-IDF = TF × IDF.
advantages: No training required beyond fitting vectorizer
limitations: No semantic understanding (synonyms treated as different words)
real_world_example:
```python
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline

# Classic text classification pipeline
pipeline = Pipeline([
    ('tfidf', TfidfVectorizer(
        max_features=10000,
        ngram_range=(1, 2),      # Unigrams and bigrams
        min_df=2,                # Ignore very rare terms
        max_df=0.95,             # Ignore very common terms
        sublinear_tf=True        # Apply log to TF
    )),
    ('clf', LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000))
])
pipeline.fit(X_train_texts, y_train)
```

title: T5
definition: A Google encoder-decoder transformer model that frames all NLP tasks as text-to-text problems. Translation, summarization, classification, and QA are all handled identically: input text → output text.
why_used: Unified framework where the same model and training procedure applies to all tasks. Excellent for summarization, translation, and any generation task.
interview_point: T5 uses a prefix to specify the task: 'translate English to German: The house is wonderful' or 'summarize: [text]'. This makes it an extremely flexible model. Fine-tuning on a new task just requires adding the right prefix.
advantages: Handles any NLP task as text-to-text
limitations: Encoder-decoder is heavier than encoder-only for classification
Slower than BERT for classification tasks
real_world_example:
```python
from transformers import T5Tokenizer, T5ForConditionalGeneration

tokenizer = T5Tokenizer.from_pretrained('t5-base')
model = T5ForConditionalGeneration.from_pretrained('t5-base')

# Summarization example
text = 'summarize: Machine learning is a method of data analysis that automates analytical model building.'
inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors='pt', max_length=512, truncation=True)

outputs = model.generate(
    inputs['input_ids'],
    max_new_tokens=100,
    num_beams=4,        # Beam search for better quality
    early_stopping=True
)
print(tokenizer.decode(outputs[0], skip_special_tokens=True))
```

title: Sentence-BERT
definition: A modification of BERT using siamese and triplet network structures to derive semantically meaningful sentence embeddings. Unlike BERT, which requires running the full model for all sentence pairs, SBERT produces independent embeddings that can be compared with cosine similarity.
why_used: Reduces BERT's sentence-pair comparison from 65 hours to 5 seconds for 10,000 sentences. Fundamental to semantic search, clustering, and RAG systems.
interview_point: Vanilla BERT is slow for semantic search: finding the most similar sentence from 10K sentences requires 10K forward passes. SBERT generates one embedding per sentence — then you just do a single matrix multiplication to compare all embeddings at once using cosine similarity.
advantages: Efficient semantic similarity (one pass per sentence)
Great for semantic search and clustering
limitations: Fine-tuned embeddings are needed for domain-specific tasks
Sentence length still limited by base model
real_world_example:
```python
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer, util

model = SentenceTransformer('all-MiniLM-L6-v2')  # Fast, good quality

sentences = [
    'Machine learning is a subset of AI',
    'Deep learning uses neural networks',
    'The cat sat on the mat',
]

# Encode all sentences at once
embeddings = model.encode(sentences, convert_to_tensor=True)

# Semantic similarity matrix
cos_sim = util.cos_sim(embeddings, embeddings)
print(cos_sim)

# Find most similar pair
for i in range(len(sentences)):
    for j in range(i+1, len(sentences)):
        print(f'({i},{j}) score: {cos_sim[i][j]:.4f}')
```
